There's the other W that I'm forgetting but you understand the four W's that that lead into this. So the next one is how. Like how do we actually do this and so that's what we're gonna break down here. This is gonna be the nitty-gritty portion of this training. Okay so business intelligence metrics defined. Okay so these are the metrics bucks it's I went over it briefly before which is for any new revenue stream you're gonna want to have acquisition sets, the film is that's in financial steps. Alright so for what we're doing which is retail which is gonna cover all three. Cool so acquisition metrics leads and when I say leads we're talking about leads from paid ads. You can drill this one down into leads from organic but for the purposes of calculating acquisition costs etc. I'm money spent including test and failed ads. The reason I'm being extremely particular following thing which is gonna be their cost per lead. So when you're trying to figure out what your cost per lead is I don't want you to look at what Google, what Facebook, whatever platform says etc. The way to calculate actual ad spend is to look at how much was spent from this part of the month to this part of the month total that includes the tests and the failed ads that did not work because all of those things fact into your actual effective ad spend and then subsequently your cost per lead. And so the way to actually calculate cost per lead is not by looking at spent over this period of time divided by the number of leads I received period that's your cost per lead. Alright scheduled is gonna be the number of scheduled at the end of the month or the end of the week or whatever cycle you were using for reporting data so that you can know exactly how many appointments were scheduled. If someone cancels that for next so that the stats are cleaner okay. I'm being pretty like definitions that's cancellations remove divided by total leads okay. If someone twice say that okay. Showed is gonna be the number of people who actually show up by the total scheduled okay. So if you have 10 people show up when we scheduled lay pay that is not a sale that is a let's cross our fingers and hope. The number we just said divided by total shows. This will give you the real actual closing percentage not what your sales manager wants or not what you feel like you want because of ego or whatever. Like these are gonna be your real stats and we got 25 sales I'm gonna say 21 sales so for math sake so I got contracts with transaction successfully processed. Alright dropouts percent dropout okay. Now the next one is net starts so that's gonna be your total sales minus number of dropouts. So this is kind of a reverse number and we're gonna use both of these numbers in the forecasting that's why I'm defining both of them. So if you have 10 total sales and two people drop out then eight is your net starts. Cost of cost per acquisition CPA either of those terms are cost of ad spend so for the month your total ad spend $800 on ads and you get eight sales two people cancel so you had eight so your actual cost per acquisition of someone beginning the program is going to be $100 okay and that is how you define cost per acquisition. First visit so if someone is signing up for a $500 program and they put $199 down then the cash upfront would be simply what was successfully processed. Now one of the common questions that come up from this is what if someone does do a delayed pay and says they'll pay on Friday and today's Wednesday and then on Friday the poll on Friday and that cash would go to the acquisition portion for intelligence metrics around service and we'll get to the next one.
